meta.setting: Vertical mine shaft drops, cribbed collars, rope ladders, windlass frames, bad air, and loose rock around hard-rock claims
meta.rdr_clone_target: RDR-clone western with mine hazard, rescue pressure, hidden evidence, labor negligence, and close-quarters fear

research_grounding.title: NPS Whiskeytown History and Culture
research_grounding.use: Mining, settlement labor, and extractive pressure in the Whiskeytown region.

research_grounding.title: California State Parks Shasta State Historic Park
research_grounding.use: Gold-rush mining community context and period material culture.

generation_prompt.task: Generate the Shaft Drops region page for the 1899 Jefferson State western packet. This JSON contains shape and guidance only.
page_shape: H3 local marker line in the register of Cribbed Collar, Rope Burn, and Bad Air
square image reference immediately after the marker line
opening blockquote built as three short generated lines: shaft object, sound or air detail, plain table meaning
two compact paragraphs establishing shaft drops as mine hazard, rescue site, evidence pocket, fear test, and labor-pressure scene
closing blockquote that gives a generated shaft-drop sign and practical table meaning
generation_guidance: Generate shaft drops as dangerous working mine features: cribbed collars, windlass frame, rope ladder, bucket scars, loose rock, lamp smoke, bad air, and a dark bottom that changes every decision.
Give the table hooks around a missing miner, cut rope fibers, hidden papers below, unsafe bracing, a dropped payroll box, a rescue argument, or a foreman ordering work to continue.
Make vertical danger practical; the page should guide light, sound, time, rope handling, fall risk, air quality, and what evidence survives at the collar.
Let company neglect and worker knowledge matter without turning the page into finished backstory.
generation_prompt.hand_cut_note: The shaft should feel like a hole that charges interest on every mistake.
generation_prompt.table_spark: A shaft-drop scene should ask who tied the rope, who heard the fall, what is at the bottom, and who wants the rescue delayed.
must_preserve: Use 1899 western mining and labor language only.
The generated page must include image path images/shaft-drops.jpg.
The prompt must not contain final prose, final blockquotes, or finished field marks.
avoid: do not mention any prior packet, inherited taxonomy, or non-western source language
do not make the drop supernatural, heroic, or symbolic instead of physically dangerous
do not include modern mine safety gear, electric lamps, steel cage elevators, plastic helmets, warning tape, or clean museum staging
self_check: The future markdown follows the exact heading sequence.
The future page is about shaft drops as mine hazards, evidence sites, rescue problems, and scene engines.
The prompt contains no finished page prose.
All language belongs to grounded 1899 Jefferson State western play.
